ground_truth.title: Go Learnings
ground_truth.main_content: Getting back into go after trying it briefly five years ago (pre-generics). Here's what I've learned so far. Keep in mind, I'm (mostly) coming from a super flexible language like Typescript. I imagine coming from Python might be a little similar. Go developers are called Gophers. Go as a language is still pretty young (~15 years, still version 1.x), and it's still undergoing active development. They have introduced breaking changes without bumping the major version. Go Philosophy includes open source by default, simplicity in syntax and features, visible and easy concurrency, built-in tooling, comprehensive standard library, and distributed module distribution. Go returns errors rather than throwing (panicking). A single module is a directory with a go.mod file. A package is a directory and its contents within a module. Things that are capitalized are public. You can only access things that are package-private within a single package. Go doesn't really do inheritance. What it does instead is embedding, which is a form of composition with syntactic sugar. Go has real pointers (memory addresses), just like C. Go does not allow pointer arithmetic. Interfaces only define methods (not properties). Interface instances are ALWAYS pointers to structs. Go doesn't have constructors. But, there is a convention for constructor-like functions with are prefixed with New*. Slices and arrays look a lot alike. A slice looks like an array without a size which it kind of is. Everything is mutable. Every variable and struct property can be set/reassigned. Go doesn't have function overloading. You can't call functions outside of a function body. You can declare and initialize variables at the top-level of a file outside a function body. A type definition creates a new type that cannot be used in place of the original type. A type alias can be used interchangeably with the type it's aliasing. Go didn't originally have generics. Methods can't be generic, only structs, functions, and interfaces. Before Go 1.22, it was unsafe to use loop variables in closures. From 1.22 on, loop variables are safe to use in closures. Unlike JS, the order that keys are added to a map doesn't matter. If you iterate over map keys, they'll be in alphabetical order. Go has panic which is like throw or raise in other languages. But it's not the preferred way to handle exceptions. The recommended approach to errors is to return them from the function instead of panicking. Go lets you embed assets in your binary. But they have to be in the same directory or a child directory of the module that embeds the asset.
with: Keep in mind, I'm (mostly) coming from a super flexible language like Typescript.
Go as a language is still pretty young (~15 years, still version 1.x), and it's still undergoing active development.
Go doesn't really do inheritance. What it does instead is "embedding", which is a form of composition with syntactic sugar.
Interfaces only define methods (not properties). Interface instances are ALWAYS pointers to structs.
Go doesn't have constructors. But, there is a convention for constructor-like functions with are prefixed with New*.
